title: Fritto misto di mare
subtitle: Mixed fried seafood with lemon
excerpt: Squid, prawns, and whitebait in a light flour coating, fried fast and hot in abundant oil. The coating is barely there: a thin dusting, not a thick batter, and the seafood stays juicy inside while the outside crisps in seconds. Eat immediately with nothing but lemon and flaked salt.
sourceNotes: Fritto misto di mare is documented throughout Italian coastal cooking from the Veneto south to Sicily, with regional variations in the specific seafood and the coating (semolina, rice flour, or plain flour). Artusi describes fried fish preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain) and notes the importance of oil temperature and frying in small batches. The Venetian tradition of fritto misto uses a light dusting of flour without egg; the Ligurian version sometimes adds a thin batter.

content.text: The secret of good fritto misto is oil temperature and small batches. Too much seafood at once drops the oil temperature and the coating absorbs fat rather than crisping. The oil should be at 180°C: a cube of bread dropped in should sizzle vigorously and turn golden in 20 seconds. Fry in batches of a single layer, never more.

The coating is a light dusting of flour, nothing more. Season the flour generously. Shake off the excess before the seafood goes in. No egg, no batter: Italian fritto misto is not fish and chips. The coating exists to create a thin, crackling exterior; the seafood does the rest.

attrs.title: Eat immediately
attrs.body: Fried seafood cannot wait. The coating softens within minutes of leaving the oil. Have everything ready at the table before you start frying. Drain briefly on kitchen paper, season with flaked salt while hot, and serve portion by portion as each batch comes out of the oil.

items.prepNote: cleaned, bodies cut into 1 cm rings, tentacles left whole
or use fine semolina for extra crunch
or any neutral oil with a high smoke point
generously, in the flour and for serving

content.text: Pat the {{squid}}, {{prawns-raw}} prawns, and {{whitebait}} completely dry with kitchen paper — moisture is the enemy of crispness. Season the {{plain-flour}} generously with salt in a wide, shallow bowl. Pour the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil into a deep, heavy pan to a depth of at least 6 cm. Heat over medium-high heat to 180°C. If you do not have a thermometer, drop in a small piece of bread: it should bubble vigorously and turn golden in 20 seconds.

Working with one type of seafood at a time, toss a small batch in the flour until lightly coated. Shake off the excess vigorously in a sieve or with your hands. Lower carefully into the hot oil and fry for 1 to 2 minutes for squid rings and whitebait, 2 to 3 minutes for prawns, until pale golden and crisp. Do not crowd the pan. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain briefly on kitchen paper. Season immediately with flaked salt. Keep warm in a low oven while you fry the remaining batches.
Serve all the fried seafood together on a large plate or bowl lined with paper. Put the {{lemon}} wedges alongside. No sauce: good fritto misto needs nothing but lemon and salt.

items.symptom: Coating is greasy rather than crisp
items.cause: Oil not hot enough, or too much seafood added at once
items.fix: Bring oil back to 180°C between batches; fry in small quantities so the oil temperature does not drop

items.cause: Either severely undercooked (less than 1 minute) or overcooked (more than 3 minutes) — squid skips tender
items.fix: Fry squid rings for exactly 1 to 2 minutes at 180°C; they cook fast and must come out while still pale gold

items.symptom: Flour coating falls off in the oil
items.cause: Seafood was wet when floured, or excess flour not shaken off
items.fix: Dry thoroughly before flouring; shake off all excess flour before lowering into the oil

items.cause: Oil overheated beyond its smoke point, or wrong oil used
items.fix: Use a neutral oil with a high smoke point (vegetable, sunflower, groundnut); keep temperature at 180°C

content.text: Fritto misto di mare is a dish of the Italian coast from Venice to Palermo, with no single regional claim. Every port, every fishing village, every seaside trattoria has its version. Venice's version uses the tiny seafood of the lagoon: schie (grey prawns), moleche (soft-shell crab in season), baby squid — and fries them in fresh sunflower oil until barely golden. The Ligurian version often includes vegetables alongside the seafood. The Sicilian version adds small whole fish and sardines. The common thread is always the same: oil very hot, coating very light, eating very fast.
